p_question-3903.ja: 以下は、作家が書いたエッセイである。 エッセイのネタのほとんどを、私は日常の中から探している。もちろん「エッセイに書いて面白いネタ」と「書いてもあまり面白くないネタ」とがある。面白いものを [41] 。 つまり君は、自分が書くエッセイは全部面白いと言いたいのかね？ という声が聞こえるので、さらに説明すると、面白いかどうかは、まずは「自分にとって」である。自分にとって面白いとはどういうことか。それは私の場合、「自分がそれまで知らなかったこと」を書く、 [42] 。 知識というよりは感覚や感情であることが多い。今まで見えなかったものが見えたとき。同じ場所なのに違う場所のように感じられたとき。ずっと眠っていた記憶。思いがけない嬉うれしさや悲かなしさや淋さびしさ。不意に自分の中に生まれた、世界というものへのあらたな認識。 春になったら暖かくなるとか、困っている人には親切にしたほうがいいとか、不倫ふりんはしないほうがいいとか、梅に鴬うぐいすとか月に雁かりがねとか、すでに知っていることは、いくら上手に書いてもつまらない。このエッセイの、665文字という短さの中ですら、書いている途中で退屈になってきてしまう。 エッセイにかぎらず、 [43] でもそうだ。理想を言えば、知らないことを「知った」という報告ではなくて、この世の謎、自分自身の謎に近づいていく過程を書いていきたいと思っているし、そのような書きかたをしているときが、結局、私は一番面白いのだ。ある意味これは書くことにかぎらず、人生全般に対して採用したいと思っている [44] 。
p_question-3903.en: The following is an essay written by an author. Most of my essay ideas come from everyday life. Of course, there are “topics that make for interesting essays” and “topics that aren’t so interesting when written about.” I want to choose the interesting ones for [41] . I can already hear someone saying, “So you mean every essay you write is interesting?” To explain further: whether something is interesting is, first of all, a matter of “for me.” What does “interesting for me” mean? In my case, it means writing about “something I didn’t know before,” [42] . More often than not, it’s less knowledge than sensation or emotion. Moments when I see what I couldn’t see before. When the same place feels like a different place. Long-asleep memories. Unexpected joy, sadness, or loneliness. A newly born awareness within me of what the world is. Things we already know—like how it gets warm in spring, that we should be kind to people in trouble, that it’s better not to have affairs, “plum blossoms with nightingales” or “the moon with wild geese”—are boring no matter how well you write about them. Even within the brevity of this 665-character essay, I find myself getting bored halfway through when I write such things. And this isn’t limited to essays; it’s true for [43] as well. Ideally, rather than reporting “I learned something I didn’t know,” I want to write the very process of approaching the mysteries of the world and of myself, and when I write in that way, that’s when I ultimately find it most interesting. In a sense, this is a [44] I’d like to adopt not only for writing but for life as a whole.
p_question-3903.ko: 다음은 한 작가가 쓴 에세이다. 내 에세이 아이디어는 대개 일상에서 나온다. 물론 "에세이로 쓰면 재미있는 소재"와 "써도 별로 재미없는 소재"가 있다. [41] 재미있는 쪽을 고르고 싶다. "그럼 네가 쓰는 에세이는 다 재미있다는 거냐"는 소리가 들리는 듯하다. 더 설명하자면, 재미있는지 여부는 우선 "나에게" 재미있는가다. "나에게 재미있다"는 무슨 의미인가? 내 경우엔 "전에 몰랐던 무언가", [42] 에 대해 쓰는 것이다. 그것은 지식보다 감각이나 감정인 경우가 더 많다. 전에 보지 못하던 것을 보는 순간. 같은 장소가 다른 장소처럼 느껴지는 순간. 오래 잠들었던 기억. 예상치 못한 기쁨·슬픔·외로움. 세상이 무엇인지에 대한 새로운 자각. 봄이 따뜻해진다거나, 곤란한 사람에겐 친절해야 한다거나, 바람피우지 않는 게 좋다거나, "매화에 휘파람새" "달에 기러기"같이 이미 아는 일은 아무리 잘 써도 재미없다. 665자짜리 짧은 에세이 안에서도 그런 것을 쓰면 도중에 지루해진다. 에세이뿐만 아니라 [43] 에서도 그렇다. 이상적인 건 "몰랐던 것을 알았다"는 보고가 아니라, 세계와 나 자신의 신비에 다가가는 그 과정을 쓰는 것이다. 그렇게 쓸 때가 결국 가장 재미있다. 이는 어떤 의미에서 글쓰기뿐만 아니라 인생 전반에 대해 채택하고 싶은 [44].

p_question-3911.ja: (1) 機械はどんどん精密化し、巨大に進化している。人間の介入を許さないかのような迫力さえ感じさせる。だが、どれほど機械が進化しようとも、本質は変わらない。熟練工(注1)が金かな槌やドライバーなどの道具を、自分の体の一部のように自在に使いこなすことで最大のパフォーマンスを発揮するように、精密機器や巨大な設備も、人間が活いき活いきと動かすことが必要である。機械も道具である、という当たり前のことを、私たちは忘れてはいないだろうか。人間が機械に使われたり、単なる番人(注2)になったりするようなことがあってはいけない。 （注1）熟練工：熟練した職人 （注2）番人：監視する人
p_question-3911.en: (1) Machines keep becoming more precise and evolving to massive scales—so imposing that they almost seem to allow no human intervention. Yet no matter how machines evolve, their essence does not change. Just as a skilled craftsperson (Note 1) achieves maximum performance by freely using tools like hammers and screwdrivers as if they were part of their own body, precision instruments and large facilities also need to be operated in a lively, human way. Have we forgotten the obvious truth that machines are tools? People must not be used by machines or reduced to mere guards (Note 2). (Note 1) Skilled craftsperson: an experienced artisan (Note 2) Guard: a person who monitors
p_question-3911.ko: (1) 기계는 점점 정밀해지고 거대화되며 인간이 개입할 여지가 없어 보일 만큼 위압적으로 진화한다. 그러나 기계가 어떻게 진화해도 그 본질은 변하지 않는다. 숙련된 장인(주1)이 망치·드라이버 같은 도구를 자기 몸의 일부처럼 자유로이 다뤄 최대 성능을 끌어내듯, 정밀기기와 대규모 설비도 사람답고 활기 있게 운전해야 한다. 기계가 도구라는 당연한 사실을 잊은 게 아닐까. 사람이 기계에게 부려지거나 단순한 감시자(주2)로 전락해선 안 된다. (주1) 숙련된 장인: 경험 많은 장인 (주2) 감시자: 지켜보는 사람

p_question-3912.ja: (2) 以下は、ある電気店から届いたメールである。 宛て先：syo_yasuhara@kfy.co.jp 件 名：イヤホン「AS-10」の件 日 時：9月13日 13：30 安原 正一様 LM電気大木店をご利用いただき、ありがとうございます。 （続きの部分） ご予約いただいたイヤホン「AS-10」ですが、メーカーの生産が遅れているため、発売日（9月20日）当日に、すべてのお客様にお渡しすることが困難な状況です。 本日、当店で9月20日にお渡しできる数が確定し、安原様のご注文分は確保できないことが分かりました。大変申し訳ございません。 安原様へのお渡しは10月以降になってしまうのですが、いかがいたしましょうか。ご注文のキャンセルも承っております。 お忙しいところ恐縮ですが、ご返信お待ちしております。 LM電気 大木店 担当：上田 映子

p_question-3912.ko: (2) 다음은 가전 매장에서 보낸 메일이다. 받는이: syo_yasuhara@kfy.co.jp 제목: 이어폰 「AS-10」 관련 일시: 9/13 13:30 안녕하세요 야스하라 쇼이치 님, LM Electric 오기점을 이용해 주셔서 감사합니다. (계속) 예약하신 이어폰 「AS-10」가 제조사 생산 지연으로 출시일(9/20)에 모든 고객께 제공이 어려운 상황입니다. 오늘 9/20 당점에서 인도 가능한 수량이 확정되었는데 야스하라 님 분량은 확보되지 않았습니다. 진심으로 사과드립니다. 인도는 10월 이후가 되는데 어떻게 진행하시겠습니까? 주문 취소도 가능합니다. 바쁘신 중에 죄송합니다만 회신 부탁드립니다. LM Electric 오기점 담당: 우에다 에이코

p_question-3913.ja: (3) 消費者ニーズの多様化が進む今日、「あらゆる人のための商品」はありえない。経営資源が限られるひとつの企業が、すべての顧客を満足させることは不可能だ。 今の時代、すべての顧客の要望に応えようとすると、結局、誰の要望にも応えないことになってしまう。「万人受け」(注)という言葉はすでに死語かもしれない。 「誰に売らないか」を決めることによって、経営資源を最大限に有効に活用し、「売るべき人」に集中することができる。 （注）万人受け：皆に受け入れられること
p_question-3913.en: (3) Today, as consumer needs diversify, a “product for everyone” cannot exist. It is impossible for a single company with limited resources to satisfy all customers. In our times, trying to meet every customer’s request ends up meeting no one’s. The phrase “appeals to everyone” (Note) may already be obsolete. By deciding “whom not to sell to,” you can use management resources to maximum effect and focus on “the people you should sell to.” (Note) Appeals to everyone: being accepted by all
p_question-3913.ko: (3) 오늘날 소비자 니즈가 다양해지면서 "모든 사람을 위한 상품"은 존재할 수 없다. 자원이 한정된 한 회사가 모든 고객을 만족시키는 건 불가능하다. 모든 고객 요구를 맞추려다 결국 누구도 만족 못 시키는 시대다. "모두에게 어필"(주)이라는 말은 이미 시대에 뒤떨어진 표현일지 모른다. "누구에게 안 팔지"를 정함으로써 경영자원을 최대한 활용하고 "팔아야 할 사람"에 집중할 수 있다. (주) 모두에게 어필: 모든 사람에게 받아들여지는 것

p_question-3914.ja: 「自分の悪い部分を露あらわにすると、嫌われたり、敬遠されたりするのではないか」という不安は、もちろんある。しかし、世間からの評価や期待に対して神経質になりすぎ、そのせいで常に不安を抱えながら生きていくならば、他人から嫌われるほうがよほどましである。 そもそも他人は、あなたが思っているほどあなたに対して期待などしていない。誰もが皆、自分のことで頭がいっぱいで、他人のことなど気にかけてはいない。
p_question-3914.en: Of course there is the anxiety that “if I expose my bad sides, I might be disliked or shunned.” However, if you become overly sensitive to society’s evaluations and expectations and end up living in constant anxiety because of that, it is far better to be disliked by others. In the first place, other people do not have as many expectations of you as you think. Everyone’s head is full of their own concerns, and they don’t pay much attention to others.
p_question-3914.ko: 물론 "안 좋은 면을 드러내면 미움받거나 따돌림 당할까" 하는 불안이 있다. 그러나 사회의 평가·기대에 과민해져 늘 불안 속에 사는 것보다, 차라리 미움받는 편이 훨씬 낫다. 애초에 사람들은 당신에게 그렇게 많은 기대를 하지 않는다. 모두 자기 일로 머리가 가득해 남에 대해선 별 신경 쓰지 않는다.

p_question-3916.ja: (1) 中等教育(注1)で、生徒たちはいろいろな科目でさまざまな分野の知識に触れていきます。 どのような知識でも必ず前提となる世界観や物事の枠組みがあり、そうした背景なくしては該当する理論や見識が成り立ちません。 生徒たちは、それぞれの科目で分野ごとの専門知識に触れていくことによって、前提とされる世界観や物事の枠組みを、自分の世界観や考え方の一部として固定化させていきます。 つまり、学習を進めて、専門知識を身につければつけるほど、前提とされる価値観や枠組みに縛られていくことになるのです。 一方で、技術革新やグローバル化により、社会の仕組みや共通認識が、目まぐるしく変化する中、現在の自分の世界観から飛び出して、他の価値観を理解する力が必要とされています。 哲学の営みの中核は、物事の根本や前提を問い直して、考察することにあります。 哲学の営みに親しむことで、現在のものの見方や考える枠組みから自分を解き放ち、急速に変化する社会の中で、揺るぎない(注2)自分の価値観を模索していく力を身につけることができるのです。 より深いレベルでの学習が始まる中等教育においてこそ、より柔軟な「哲学する力」を養い始めることが必要とされているのです。 （注1）中等教育：中学、高校での教育 （注2）揺ゆるぎない：強固な
p_question-3916.en: (1) In secondary education (Note 1), students encounter knowledge from many fields across a variety of subjects. All knowledge has underlying worldviews and frameworks; without such backgrounds, the related theories and insights cannot stand. As students come into contact with specialized knowledge in each subject, those assumed worldviews and frameworks become fixed as part of their own worldviews and ways of thinking. In other words, the more they learn and acquire specialized knowledge, the more they become bound by the values and frameworks that are taken for granted. Meanwhile, amid rapid changes in social systems and shared understandings due to technological innovation and globalization, we need the ability to step outside our current worldviews and understand other value systems. The core of philosophical practice lies in re-examining and reflecting on fundamentals and assumptions. By engaging with philosophy, we can free ourselves from current ways of seeing and thinking, and develop the capacity to search for unwavering (Note 2) personal values in a rapidly changing society. It is precisely in secondary education—where deeper learning begins—that we are called to start cultivating a more flexible “ability to philosophize.” (Note 1) Secondary education: education in junior high and high school (Note 2) Unwavering: firm, solid
p_question-3916.ko: (1) 중등교육(주1)에선 학생이 다양한 과목에서 여러 분야의 지식과 만난다. 모든 지식에는 그 기반이 되는 세계관·틀이 있고, 그것 없이는 관련 이론·통찰이 성립하지 않는다. 학생이 각 과목의 전문 지식과 접하면서 그 전제된 세계관·틀이 자기 사고의 일부로 굳어진다. 즉 전문 지식을 익힐수록 당연시되는 가치·틀에 묶이게 된다. 한편 기술 혁신·세계화로 사회 시스템·공통 이해가 급격히 변하는 가운데, 우리는 현재의 세계관 밖으로 나가 다른 가치 체계를 이해할 능력이 필요하다. 철학적 실천의 핵심은 근본·전제를 다시 묻고 성찰하는 데 있다. 철학을 통해 현재의 사고·관점에서 자유로워지고 변화하는 사회에서 흔들리지 않는(주2) 자신의 가치를 찾을 능력을 기를 수 있다. 깊은 학습이 시작되는 중등교육 단계에서 더 유연한 "철학하는 능력"을 기를 필요가 있다. (주1) 중등교육: 중·고교 교육 (주2) 흔들리지 않는: 굳건한

p_question-3918.ja: (2) 以下は、羽毛を持つ恐竜について述べられた文章である。 恐竜には、鳥のように卵を温める習性があったことがわかっています。 （中略） 気になるのは、いつから卵を温めるようになったのかということですが、羽毛を持った時点で、卵を温める習性も持っていた可能性があります。 羽毛を持つことで、体温が維持できるようになるので、その体温を使って卵の温度を一定に保つことができます。特に夜間は気温が下がるので、夜に親が卵の上に座って眠っていれば、卵の保温にはとても効果的です。卵が一定の温かさで保たれていれば、さまざまな環境で卵が孵かえる確率が高くなります。 爬虫類はちゅうるいは卵を温めません。爬虫類はちゅうるいの卵は、放置されても、1日のうちある程度の時間、気温が30度を超えるなどの条件が整っていれば、自然と孵かえります。その代わり、爬虫類はちゅうるいは1年のうち気温の高い限られた時期にしか産卵しません。生息地域（注）も限られます。 羽毛のある恐竜が、鳥に近い体温を持っていたとすれば、夏以外の季節でも、寒冷地でも、安定して35～40度ほどの温度で卵を温めることが可能です。 厳密に言うと、羽毛があると体の熱を逃がさないので、卵を温めるには不向きです。人間で言うと、衣服の上からでは温めにくいのと同じです。温めるなら、服の中に入れて直接体温が伝わるようにするはずです。 卵を抱く時期の鳥も、卵と接する部分の羽毛がなくなり、皮膚がむき出しになります。恐竜が卵を温めていたとすれば、おそらく同じように、お腹のあたりの羽毛が抜けていたと思われます。 （注）生息地域：生活している地域
p_question-3918.en: (2) The following passage discusses dinosaurs with feathers. It is known that some dinosaurs had the habit of warming their eggs like birds do. (Omitted) A key question is when they began warming eggs; once they had feathers, they may also have developed the habit of brooding eggs. Having feathers allows body temperature to be maintained, and that body heat can keep the eggs at a constant temperature. Since temperatures drop at night, a parent sitting on the eggs while sleeping at night is very effective for keeping them warm. If eggs are kept at a steady warmth, the likelihood of hatching increases across various environments. Reptiles do not warm their eggs. Reptile eggs will hatch naturally even if left alone, provided conditions such as air temperatures exceeding 30°C for part of the day are met. In exchange, reptiles lay eggs only during limited warm periods of the year, and their habitats (Note) are restricted. If feathered dinosaurs had body temperatures close to those of birds, they could keep eggs warm at a stable 35–40°C even outside summer and even in cold regions. Strictly speaking, feathers trap body heat, so they are not ideal for warming eggs. For humans, it’s like how it’s hard to warm something over clothing; if you want to warm it, you put it under the clothes so body heat is transferred directly. In birds during the brooding period, the feathers in the area that contacts the eggs disappear, exposing the skin. If dinosaurs warmed their eggs, their belly feathers likely fell out in the same way. (Note) Habitat: the region where they live
p_question-3918.ko: (2) 깃털이 있던 공룡에 관한 글이다. 일부 공룡은 새처럼 알을 품는 습성이 있었던 것으로 알려져 있다. (중략) 핵심은 언제부터 알을 품기 시작했는가다. 깃털이 생긴 뒤 알 품기 습성도 발전했을 가능성이 있다. 깃털이 있으면 체온을 유지할 수 있고, 그 체온으로 알 온도를 일정하게 유지할 수 있다. 밤엔 기온이 떨어지므로 부모가 자며 알 위에 앉아 있는 것은 알을 따뜻하게 하는 데 매우 효과적이다. 알이 일정 온도로 유지되면 다양한 환경에서 부화 가능성이 커진다. 파충류는 알을 품지 않는다. 파충류 알은 하루 일정 시간 30도 이상이면 자연 부화한다. 대신 1년 중 따뜻한 한정된 시기에만 알을 낳고, 서식지(주)도 한정된다. 깃털 공룡이 새와 비슷한 체온이었다면 여름 외 시기·추운 지역에서도 35-40도로 알을 안정적으로 따뜻하게 유지할 수 있었다. 엄밀히는 깃털은 체열을 가두므로 알을 직접 데우는 데 이상적이지 않다. 사람으로 치면 옷 위에서 데우긴 어려우니 옷 안에 두는 식이다. 새는 포란 시기에 알에 닿는 부위의 깃털이 빠져 피부가 노출된다. 공룡이 알을 품었다면 배 깃털도 비슷하게 빠졌을 것이다. (주) 서식지: 사는 지역

p_question-3920.ja: (3) 以下は、仕事でリーダーの立場にある人に向けて書かれた文章である。 「君は何がやりたいの？」「どんな仕事が好きですか？」と聞いたとき、本人の口から出てくる答えが本当に「向いていること」とは限りません。なぜなら、どんな仕事をやりたいかについては、驚くほど多くの人がイメージに左右されています。特に若い人や新人であれば、その傾向は強くなります。本人の発言を鵜呑うのみにして(注1)はいけないのです。 仕事の実情を知らずに単純に「あの仕事が好きだ！」と思い込んでいたり、「商品開発のAさんは楽しそう。私も商品開発をやりたい」と憧れていたり。上司に何をやりたいか聞かれたから、それほど強い興味があるわけではなくても「特にありません」と答えるのは気まずいので、「なんとなくやりたいもの」をとりあえず答えただけというケースもあります。 それを踏まえずに、「君、広報が好きなの？ じゃあ、やってみなさい！ 自分で言うならモチベーション(注2)も高いからうまくいくだろう」というリーダーは、マネジメント(注3)という大切な仕事を放棄しているようなものです。 本人も気づかない埋もれたスキルを引き出し、チームの勝利に貢献してもらうには、リーダーがメンバー自身よりも、その人の適性を把握していなければなりません。 （中略） 本当に適性があれば、新しいポジションで成果を出します。成果が出ると面白くなり、ますますスキルが上がります。やがて「自分が貢献できている、チームの役に立っている」と実感できるようになれば、それがそのメンバーのやりたい仕事になっていきます。 （注1）鵜呑うのみにする：そのまま受け入れる （注2）モチベーション：意欲 （注3）マネジメント：ここでは、チームのメンバーの管理
p_question-3920.en: (3) The following passage is addressed to people in leadership positions at work. When you ask, “What do you want to do?” or “What kind of work do you like?”, the answer a person gives is not necessarily what they are truly suited for. That’s because, when it comes to what job they want to do, surprisingly many people are swayed by images and impressions. This tendency is especially strong among young people and newcomers. You must not take their words at face value (Note 1). Some may naively be convinced “I like that job!” without knowing the reality of the work, or they may admire someone—“Person A in product development looks like they’re having fun; I want to do product development too.” Others, when asked by a superior what they want to do, feel awkward saying “nothing in particular” if they aren’t strongly interested, so they just name something they “kind of” want to do for the moment. If you ignore that and say, “So, you like PR? Then give it a try! Since you said so yourself, your motivation (Note 2) is high, so it should go well,” as a leader you are essentially abandoning the important job of management (Note 3). To uncover buried skills the person hasn’t noticed and have them contribute to the team’s success, leaders must grasp each member’s aptitudes even better than the members themselves do. (Omitted) If someone truly has the aptitude, they will produce results in the new position. As results come, the work becomes enjoyable and their skills improve further. Eventually, when they can feel “I’m contributing; I’m useful to the team,” that becomes the work they want to do. (Note 1) To take at face value: to accept as is (Note 2) Motivation: willingness, drive (Note 3) Management: here, the management of team members
p_question-3920.ko: (3) 다음은 직장 리더 위치 사람을 향한 글이다. "무엇을 하고 싶나?" "어떤 일이 좋나?"라는 물음에 대한 답이 그 사람이 정말 적성 있는 일이라곤 할 수 없다. 어떤 일을 하고 싶은지에 대해선 의외로 많은 사람이 이미지·인상에 좌우되기 때문이다. 특히 젊은 사람·신입에게 강하다. 그들의 말을 그대로(주1) 받아들여선 안 된다. 어떤 이는 일의 실제를 모른 채 "저 일이 좋아!"라 단순히 확신하거나, 동경하며 "제품 개발 A씨가 즐거워 보이니 나도 제품 개발하고 싶다"라 한다. 또 어떤 이는 상사가 물을 때 "딱히 없어요"라 답하기 어색해 임시로 적당히 하고 싶다는 일을 댄다. 그것을 무시하고 "홍보 좋아? 그럼 해봐. 본인이 그렇게 말했으니 동기(주2)도 높을 거야, 잘될 거야"라고 한다면 리더로서 매니지먼트(주3)라는 중요한 일을 포기한 셈이다. 본인이 자각 못 한 묻혀 있는 능력을 발굴해 팀에 기여하게 하려면, 리더는 멤버 본인보다 멤버의 적성을 더 잘 파악해야 한다. (중략) 정말 적성 있는 일이라면 새 보직에서 결과가 나온다. 결과가 나면 일이 즐거워지고 능력이 더 자란다. 그리고 "내가 기여하고 있다, 팀에 도움이 된다"고 느낄 수 있게 되면 그것이 본인이 하고 싶은 일이 된다. (주1) 그대로: 있는 그대로 받아들이다 (주2) 동기: 의욕 (주3) 매니지먼트: 여기서는 팀원 관리

p_question-3922.ja: (4) 実験科学の世界では、仮説にぴたりと合致するような結果が得られることはまずないといってよい。その際、ほとんどの研究者はこう考える。自分の仮説は間違っていない。ただ、実験の方法がよくないから、よいデータが出ないのだと。そこで条件を少しずつ変えて、繰り返し実験を行うことになる。しかし、ほとんどの場合、実験がうまくいかないのは、実は、仮説そのものが間違っているからなのだ。 だが、研究者は頑迷がんめい(注1)なので自説に固執してしまう。かくして膨大な時間と試行錯誤が浪費される。なので、科学研究にほんとうに必要な才能は、天才性やひらめきというよりは、むしろ、自己懐疑、失望に対する耐性(注2)、潔い諦め、といったものとなる。 逆に、実験科学の世界では、時として、思い描いたとおりの、いや、想像以上にすばらしい、見事な実験データが得られることがある。こんな時、研究者に求められることは何か。ぬか喜び(注3)してはならぬ、ということである。実験の方法に穴があるから、見せかけだけの結果が出ているのかもしれない。つまりここでも自己懐疑、（希望に対する）耐性、諦め、が必要となる。 英語にはこんな言い方がある。too good to be true（できすぎは、真実ではない）。もう少しだけ研究者に冷静さがあればあの「発見」はなかった。そんな誤診ごしん((注4)はいくつでもある。 （注1）頑迷がんめい：頑固 （注2）〜に対する耐性：ここでは、〜に影響されない強さ （注3）ぬか喜び：あとでがっかりする結果になる一時的な喜び （注4）誤診ごしん：誤り
p_question-3922.en: (4) In experimental science, it is fair to say that results rarely match a hypothesis exactly. In such cases, most researchers think this way: my hypothesis isn’t wrong; it’s just that the experimental method is poor, so good data aren’t coming out. They then change conditions little by little and repeat the experiments. However, in most cases, the real reason experiments fail is that the hypothesis itself is wrong. Researchers, however, can be obstinate (Note 1) and cling to their own theories. Thus enormous amounts of time and trial and error are wasted. Therefore, the talents truly needed in scientific research are not genius or flashes of insight, but rather self-doubt, resilience to disappointment (Note 2), and the grace to give up. Conversely, in experimental science, there are times when you obtain results exactly as imagined—or even more marvelous than imagined. What is required of researchers then? Do not rejoice too soon (Note 3). A flaw in the experimental method may be producing merely apparent results. In other words, here too you need self-doubt, resilience (against hope), and resignation. There is an English saying: “too good to be true.” If researchers had been a little calmer, that “discovery” would not have happened. There are countless such errors (Note 4). (Note 1) Obstinate: stubborn (Note 2) Resistance to ~: here, the strength not to be swayed by ~ (Note 3) Premature joy: a brief joy that later ends in disappointment (Note 4) Error
p_question-3922.ko: (4) 실험 과학에서 가설과 결과가 정확히 일치하는 일은 드물다. 그럴 때 대부분 연구자는 이렇게 생각한다: "내 가설이 틀린 게 아니라 실험 방법이 미흡해 좋은 데이터가 안 나온다." 그래서 조건을 조금씩 바꿔 실험을 반복한다. 그러나 대부분 실험이 실패하는 진짜 이유는 가설 자체가 틀렸기 때문이다. 그러나 연구자는 고집 세서(주1) 자기 이론에 매달린다. 그 결과 막대한 시간·시행착오가 낭비된다. 그래서 과학 연구에 진정 필요한 재능은 천재성이나 영감이 아니라 자기 의심·실망에 굴하지 않는 강인함(주2)·포기할 수 있는 도량이다. 반대로 실험 과학에선 상상대로—혹은 그 이상으로 멋진—결과가 나오는 때도 있다. 그때 연구자에게 필요한 것은? 너무 빨리 기뻐하지 말 것(주3). 실험 방법의 결함이 외관상 결과를 만들어내고 있을 수 있다. 즉 여기서도 자기 의심·강인함(희망에 대한)·체념이 필요하다. 영어 속담 "too good to be true"가 있다. 연구자가 좀 더 차분했다면 그 "발견"이 일어나지 않았을 수도. 그런 오류(주4)는 무수히 많다. (주1) 고집 세다 (주2) 강인함: 휘둘리지 않는 힘 (주3) 너무 일찍 기뻐하지 말 것 (주4) 오류, 잘못

p_question-3924.ja: 以下は、報道写真を撮る人が書いた文章である。 写真の発表の場が減ったことで、その未来を懸念する声が多いが、私は、そうは思わない。音も動きもなく、数千分の一秒という高速で動きを止める写真は、テレビと比べると臨場感では劣り、「伝えるためのメディア」としては原始的なのかもしれない。しかし、テレビのように用意された答えを差し出し、「こうです」と押しつけるのとは違って、写真には、すべてが提示されていないからこそ、それを補うための想像力が必要となる。（中略） 写真の一瞬に込められた意味を想像すること。その瞬間の前と後、あるいは、写っていないものにまで思いをめぐらせること。さらに写っているものに、どう自分を重ね合わせ、何を感じ取るかということ。想像力を伸びやかに働かせることで、私たちは周りに流されずに、「いまの時代」を自分なりに感じ取ることができるはずだ。 しかし、想像力を呼び起こすためには、写真に「絵」としての力があることが必要条件となる。最初に「絵画」の構成力や力強さといったものがあってこそ、人を惹きつけることができるはずで、そこから何を感じてもらうかはその次のこととなる。 写真を始めた頃、「名作」と呼ばれる写真をたくさん見るように努めた。ロバート・キャパの「倒れし兵士」、カルティエ・ブレッソンの「水たまりを飛ぶ男」、ユージン・スミスの「シュバイツァ博士」……。それらの写真を前に、「どうして名作なのか」と考えた。その時は、すぐには答えが見つからなかったが、いま思うのは、「名作とは見る人が、それぞれに感じ取れるものがある写真」ということだ。悲しい時、うれしい時、その時々の心のあり様によって、一枚の写真から感じるものは違ってくる。若い時に、私が感動した写真でも、いま見ると、さほど感じないというものもあるし、逆に、若い頃は何も感じなかった写真がいまになって心に迫ってくることもある。様々な人が、心のままに写真に向き合い、そこからいろいろなメッセージをくみとれる写真が、多くの人に支持され、時を越えて残っていく名作なのだと思う。 写真の力が失われていると嘆く人もいるが、いまはカメラ付き携帯電話が普及し、多くの場所で写真を撮ることができる時代でもある。その意味では、一億総カメラマンの時代といえるのかもしれない。誰もが撮ることができる時代だからこそ、独自の視点で、他の 人が見逃していたアングル(注)で素晴らしい写真を撮ることができれば、多くの人たちによさが認めてもらえる環境が整ったといえるのではないだろうか。 （注）アングル：角度
p_question-3924.en: The following is a piece written by a news photographer. Many worry about the future of photography because there are fewer venues for presenting it, but I don’t see it that way. A photograph has no sound or motion, and freezes action at speeds of a few thousandths of a second; compared to television it may lack immediacy and seem primitive as a “medium for conveying.” Yet unlike television, which hands you a prepared answer and insists “this is how it is,” photography, precisely because not everything is shown, requires imagination to fill in the gaps. (Omitted) To imagine the meaning contained in a photographic instant. To let your thoughts range to what came before and after that moment, or even to what is not pictured. Furthermore, how you overlay yourself onto what is depicted and what you take from it. By letting imagination work freely, we should be able to sense “the present age” in our own way without being swept along by others. However, to awaken imagination, a photograph must first have the power of a “picture.” Only when there is, to begin with, the compositional strength and forcefulness of a painting can it attract people; what they then feel from it comes next. When I started photography, I tried to look at many photographs called “masterpieces”: Robert Capa’s “The Falling Soldier,” Cartier-Bresson’s “Man Jumping Over a Puddle,” W. Eugene Smith’s “Dr. Schweitzer,” and so on. In front of those photographs, I wondered, “Why are these masterpieces?” I didn’t find the answer right away then, but now I think a masterpiece is “a photograph from which each viewer can take away something of their own.” What you feel from a single photograph differs depending on your state of mind—when you’re sad, when you’re happy. Some photos that moved me when I was young don’t affect me much now, while others that meant nothing to me then now strike home. Photographs that people of many kinds can face with open hearts and draw many messages from—those are the ones widely supported and that remain across time as masterpieces. Some lament that photography has lost its power, but today camera-equipped mobile phones are widespread, and it’s an era in which photos can be taken in many places. In that sense, you could call it an age of “a hundred million photographers.” Precisely because anyone can shoot, if you can take wonderful photographs from unique perspectives and angles (Note) that others have overlooked, perhaps the conditions are now in place for many people to recognize their merit. (Note) Angle: viewpoint
p_question-3924.ko: 다음은 보도 사진가가 쓴 글이다. 사진을 발표할 무대가 줄어 사진의 미래를 걱정하는 사람이 많지만, 나는 그렇게 보지 않는다. 사진은 소리도 움직임도 없고 1/수천초 정도의 동작을 정지시킨 것이라, TV에 비하면 즉각성이 없고 "전달 매체"로서 원시적으로 보일 수 있다. 그러나 "이거다"라고 정답을 들이미는 TV와 달리, 사진은 모든 것을 보여주지 않기에 빈 곳을 메우는 상상력이 필요하다. (중략) 사진 한순간에 담긴 의미를 상상한다. 그 순간 전후, 더 나아가 사진에 찍히지 않은 것까지 생각을 펼친다. 또 자신을 사진 속 무엇에 어떻게 겹쳐 보고 무엇을 받아들일지. 상상력이 자유로이 작동할 때 우리는 남에게 휩쓸리지 않고 "지금 이 시대"를 자기만의 감각으로 느낄 수 있다. 그러나 상상력을 깨우려면 사진이 우선 "한 장의 그림"으로서 힘이 있어야 한다. 그림 같은 구도·박력이 있어야 사람을 끌고, 거기서 무엇을 느낄지가 그 다음이다. 사진을 시작했을 때 "명작"이라 불리는 사진들을 많이 봤다. 로버트 카파 「쓰러지는 병사」, 카르티에 브레송 「물웅덩이를 뛰는 남자」, W. 유진 스미스 「슈바이처 박사」 등. 그 사진 앞에서 "왜 이게 명작일까"라 생각했다. 당시엔 답을 못 찾았지만, 지금은 명작이란 "보는 사람마다 자기 것을 가져갈 수 있는 사진"이라 생각한다. 한 장의 사진에서 느끼는 것은 마음 상태에 따라 달라진다 — 슬플 때, 기쁠 때. 젊을 때 감동했던 사진이 지금은 별 감흥이 없거나, 그땐 아무것도 못 느낀 사진이 지금 와닿기도 한다. 다양한 사람이 열린 마음으로 마주해 다양한 메시지를 가져갈 수 있는 사진이 폭넓게 지지받고 시간을 넘어 명작으로 남는다. 사진의 힘이 잃어졌다 한탄하는 이도 있지만, 오늘은 카메라폰이 보급되어 어디서나 사진 찍는 시대다. "1억 명이 사진가"라 부를 수 있는 시대다. 누구나 찍을 수 있기에 남이 놓친 독자적 시점·앵글(주)로 멋진 사진을 찍을 수 있다면 많은 이가 그 가치를 알아볼 환경은 오히려 더 갖춰진 것일지 모른다. (주) 앵글: 시점

p_question-3927.ja: A 小説を書こうとしている人やすでに何作か書いたことのある人が、よく私にテクニック面での質問をしてくるけれど、そういうとき私は「あなたが技術や手法について誰かに訊きく(注1)たびに小説はあなたから離れていく」と答えることにしている。 （中略） 自分の小説の行き詰まりをテクニック不足が原因だと考える人は「小説の書き方マニュアル」を信じる律儀りちぎさ(注2)と同じで、たしかに真面目で素直ないい人ではあるのだろうが、本当に自分が書きたいことが何なのかをきちんと考えていないという意味で、急いているということになる。 小説の中身と表現・手法・技術の関係は何重にも畳たたみ込まれた(注3)もので、厳密に論じだしたらキリがない(注4)が、まずはそんな面倒なことは考えずに、「本当に書きたいことだったらテクニックなんか関係ない」と、シンプルに考えてほしい。 B 小説を書くには想像以上の気力が必要である。書くことを決めて執筆に取りかかっても、自分には技術がないから下手な文章しか書けないと手を止めてしまう人が多い。しかし、そんなことで悩んでいるくらいなら、まずはどんどん書き進めてみるべきだ。テーマとジャンルを決める、構成と展開を考える、登場人物と舞台を決めるといった必要な手順を踏んだら、あとは書き始めること、そして完結させることが何より重要なのだ。 いくら面白いストーリーでも、完結させないことには小説とはいえない。短編小説でも、納得できるできる出来栄えでなくてもかまわない。まずは自分の力で一つの作品を書き上げることだ。それができれば、自信が生まれ次の作品につながっていく。 （注1）訊きく：ここでは、質問する （注2）律儀りちぎさ：礼儀正しさ （注3）何重にも畳たたみ込まれた：ここでは、複雑に絡み合った （注4）キリがない：際限がない
p_question-3927.en: A People who are trying to write fiction—or who have already written a few pieces—often ask me about technique, but at such times I make a point of answering, “Every time you ask someone (Note 1) about skills or methods, the novel moves farther away from you.” (Omitted) Those who think their fiction’s impasse is due to a lack of technique are, in their dutifulness (Note 2) of believing in “how-to-write-a-novel manuals,” likely earnest, straightforward, good people—but in the sense that they haven’t properly considered what they truly want to write, they are being hasty. The relationship between a novel’s content and its expression, methods, and techniques is multilayered and intricately interwoven (Note 3); if you start to analyze it strictly, there’s no end to it (Note 4). First, without thinking about such troublesome matters, I want you to think simply: “If it’s truly what you want to write, technique doesn’t matter.” B Writing a novel requires more mental energy than you might imagine. Even after deciding to write and starting, many people stop, thinking they can only produce clumsy prose because they lack technique. But if you’re going to fret about that, you should first keep writing steadily. Once you’ve taken the necessary steps—deciding the theme and genre, considering structure and development, deciding on characters and setting—what matters most is to start writing and to finish. No matter how interesting a story is, unless you complete it, it cannot be called a novel. Even a short story—even if you’re not fully satisfied with the result—is fine. First, complete a single work by your own power. Once you do that, you’ll gain confidence, which will lead to the next work. (Note 1) “Ask”: here, to ask a question (Note 2) Dutifulness: being proper and polite (Note 3) Multilayered and interwoven: here, intricately intertwined (Note 4) No end to it: endless, without limit
p_question-3927.ko: A 소설을 쓰려는 사람—혹은 이미 몇 편을 쓴 사람—이 기법을 묻는다. 그때 "기술이나 방법을 누군가에게 묻는(주1) 만큼 소설은 멀어진다"고 답하기로 한다. (중략) 소설이 막힌 이유를 기술 부족이라 생각하는 사람은 "소설 작법" 매뉴얼류를 믿는 성실함(주2)으로 보아 진실하고 좋은 사람일 것이다. 그러나 자기가 정말로 쓰고 싶은 게 뭔지 충분히 안 생각했다는 점에선 조급하다. 소설의 내용·표현·방법·기법은 다층적이고 복잡하게 얽혀(주3) 있어 엄밀히 분석하면 끝이 없다(주4). 우선 그런 골치 아픈 일은 제쳐두고 "정말로 쓰고 싶은 거라면 기법은 상관없다"고 단순히 생각하길 바란다. B 소설 쓰기는 생각보다 정신력이 많이 든다. 쓰기로 마음먹고 시작해도 "기법이 부족해 서투른 글밖에 못 쓴다"며 멈추는 사람이 많다. 그러나 그것을 고민할 거라면 일단 꾸준히 쓰는 게 우선이다. 테마·장르 결정, 구성·전개 검토, 인물·배경 결정 등 필요한 단계를 거치면 가장 중요한 건 쓰기 시작해 끝맺는 것이다. 아무리 흥미로운 이야기도 완성하지 않으면 소설이라 할 수 없다. 단편이라도, 결과가 만족스럽지 않아도 좋다. 우선 자기 힘으로 한 작품을 완성하라. 그러면 자신감이 생기고 다음 작품으로 이어진다. (주1) 묻다: 질문하다 (주2) 성실함: 예의바름 (주3) 다층적·얽힘: 복잡하게 엮임 (주4) 끝이 없다

p_question-3929.ja: SNS（注1）を含むリアルタイムウェブ（注2）の本質は、時間と過程の消去にある。かつてコンテンツ（注3）の拡散には一定の時間がかかった。権威やメディアをすり抜ける必要もあった。けれどもいまや、それらの面倒をすべてすっとばし（注4）、無名の書き手が一晩で何百万もの支持者を集めることができる。それはSNSの良いところだ。 けれども人生にはトラブルがつきもの（注5）である。どれだけ誠実に生きていても、誤解や中傷に曝さらされることが必ずある。そしてそういうとき、SNSの支持はほとんど役に立たない。匿名の支持者は、トラブルの話題自体すぐに忘れてしまう。あっというまに（注6）集まった人々は、同じくあっというまに離れる。そこで継続的に助けてくれるのは、結局は面倒な人間関係に支えられた家族や友人たちだったりする。 SNSの人間関係には面倒がない。だからSNSの知人は面倒を背負ってくれない。そんなSNSでも、たしかに人生がうまく行っているときは大きな力になる。けれども、本当の困難を抱えたときは、助けにならないのだ。 これからの時代を生きるうえで、SNSのこの性格を知っておくことはとても重要なように思う。そもそも、人生の困難なるものは自分と世界のズレの表れである。自分はあることを正しいと信じるが、世界はそう思わない——そういう対立が生じたとき、困難が訪れる。だから困難そのものが悪いわけではない。むしろ、概念の発明や政治の変革は必ず困難とともに生じる。その困難を時間をかけて解消し昇華しょうかする（注7）ことで、はじめて自分も相手も社会も進歩するのだ。けれども、いまのSNSにはそのような熟成の余裕がほとんどない。 困難な時期を支えるとは、言いかえれば、支える相手と世界の関係が変化する過程に時間をかけてつきあうということである。ひとりの人間が変わるというのはたいへんなことで、「いいね！」をつけるようにポンポン複製できるものではない。いわゆる「議論」で相手が変わると考えているひとは、人間の本質について無知である。ぼくが一生をかけて変えることができるのは、ごく少数の身の回りの人々だけであり、そしてぼくを変えることができるのもおそらくは彼らだけだ。その小さく面倒な人間関係をどれだけ濃密に作れるかで、人生の広がりが決まるのだと思う。 家族も友人もあっというまには作れない。面倒な存在でもある。だからこそそれは変化の受け皿となる。面倒がないところに変化はない。情報技術は、面倒のない人間関係の調達を可能にしたが、それはまた人間から変化の可能性を奪うものでもあった。そのことを忘れずにおきたいと思う。 （注1）SNS：ウェブ上での情報のやり取りや交流の場を提供するサービス （注2）リアルタイムウェブ：情報更新が即時に行われるウェブ （注3）コンテンツ：ここでは、情報 （注4）すっとばす：ここでは、省略する （注5）つきもの：必ず伴うもの （注6）あっというまに：短い間に （注7）昇華しょうかする：ここでは、別の良いものに変える
p_question-3929.en: The essence of the real-time web (Note 2), including SNS (Note 1), is the erasure of time and process. Once, it took a certain amount of time for content (Note 3) to spread, and one had to slip past authorities and the media. But now, all that hassle can be skipped (Note 4), and an unknown writer can gather millions of supporters overnight. That is the good side of SNS. However, trouble is part and parcel of life (Note 5). No matter how sincerely you live, there will inevitably be times when you face misunderstanding or slander. And at such times, support on SNS is of little use. Anonymous supporters quickly forget the issue itself. People who gathered in a flash (Note 6) leave just as quickly. Those who continue to help you are, in the end, family and friends sustained by those troublesome human relationships. Relationships on SNS involve no hassle. Therefore acquaintances on SNS won’t take on hassle for you. Even so, SNS can indeed be a great force when life is going well. But when you face real hardship, it will not help. To live in the coming era, it seems very important to understand this character of SNS. Difficulties in life are manifestations of misalignment between oneself and the world. You believe something is right, but the world does not—when such conflict arises, difficulties come. So difficulty itself is not bad. Rather, the invention of concepts and political change always arise together with difficulties. Only by spending time resolving and sublimating them (Note 7) can you, the other party, and society progress. Yet today’s SNS has almost no room for such maturation. To support someone through a difficult period, in other words, is to take the time to stay with the process in which that person’s relationship with the world changes. For a human being to change is a tremendous thing; it’s not something that can be replicated with a quick “Like.” Those who think the other side will change through so-called “debate” are ignorant of human nature. The people I can change over a lifetime are only a very few close to me, and probably only they can change me. How densely we can build those small, troublesome human relationships determines the breadth of our lives. You cannot form family or friendships in an instant. They are troublesome, too. That is precisely why they can serve as vessels for change. Where there is no hassle, there is no change. Information technology has made it possible to obtain hassle-free human relationships, but it has also deprived humans of the possibility of change. I would like not to forget that. (Note 1) SNS: services that provide places on the web for information exchange and interaction (Note 2) Real-time web: the web where information updates occur instantly (Note 3) Content: here, information (Note 4) Skip: here, to omit the steps (Note 5) Part and parcel: something that always accompanies (Note 6) In an instant: in a short time (Note 7) Sublimate: here, to transform into something better
p_question-3929.ko: SNS(주1)를 포함한 실시간 웹(주2)의 본질은 시간·과정의 소거다. 한때는 콘텐츠(주3)가 퍼지는 데 시간이 걸렸고 권위·언론을 빠져나가야 했다. 지금은 그 모든 번거로움을 건너뛸 수 있고(주4), 무명 작가가 하룻밤에 수백만 지지자를 모을 수 있다. 그게 SNS의 좋은 면이다. 그러나 인생엔 트러블이 늘 따른다(주5). 아무리 진심으로 살아도 오해·비방을 받는 때가 반드시 온다. 그때 SNS의 응원은 큰 힘이 안 된다. 익명 응원자는 사안 자체를 금세 잊는다. 순식간에(주6) 모인 사람들은 그만큼 빨리 떠난다. 끝까지 도와주는 건 결국 번거로운 인간관계로 지탱되는 가족·친구다. SNS 관계엔 번거로움이 없다. 그래서 SNS 지인은 당신을 위해 번거로움을 떠맡지 않는다. 그래도 일이 잘될 땐 SNS가 큰 힘이 된다. 그러나 진짜 어려움 앞에선 도움이 안 된다. 다가올 시대를 살려면 SNS의 이런 성격을 이해하는 게 매우 중요해 보인다. 인생의 어려움은 자신과 세계의 어긋남의 발현이다. 자신은 옳다 믿어도 세계는 그렇지 않다 — 그런 충돌이 오면 어려움이 온다. 어려움 자체가 나쁜 건 아니다. 오히려 개념 발명·정치 변화는 늘 어려움과 함께 일어난다. 시간을 들여 그것을 풀어가고 승화(주7)할 때만 자신·상대·사회가 진보한다. 그러나 오늘날 SNS엔 그런 성숙의 여지가 거의 없다. 어려운 시기를 함께 견디는 것은, 즉 그 사람과 세계의 관계가 변하는 과정에 시간을 들여 동행하는 일이다. 인간이 변하는 건 큰 일이다. "좋아요" 한 번으로 대체할 수 없다. "논쟁"으로 상대를 바꿀 수 있다 믿는 이는 인간 본성을 모른다. 평생 내가 변화시킬 수 있는 사람은 가까운 소수뿐이고, 그들만이 나를 변화시킬 수 있다. 그 작고 번거로운 인간관계를 얼마나 촘촘히 맺느냐가 인생의 폭을 결정한다. 가족·친구는 한순간에 만들어지지 않는다. 그것도 번거롭다. 바로 그래서 변화의 그릇이 된다. 번거로움이 없는 곳엔 변화가 없다. 정보 기술이 번거로움 없는 인간관계를 가능케 했지만 동시에 인간에게서 변화의 가능성을 빼앗았다. 잊지 않고 싶다. (주1) SNS: 정보 교환·교류의 장 (주2) 실시간 웹: 즉각 갱신되는 웹 (주3) 콘텐츠: 정보 (주4) 건너뛰다 (주5) 늘 따라다님 (주6) 한순간에 (주7) 승화: 더 좋은 형태로 바꿈

p_question-3932.ja: マスダ買い取りサービス マスダ買い取りサービス 買い取りサービスのご利用について ◆買い取り可能な品物 冷蔵庫などの家電、机などの家具、自転車、楽器を受け付けています。詳しくは、「買い取り可能な品物」のページをご確認ください。 ◆買い取り方法と流れ 店頭買取 ① 店頭へお持ちください。 ② 品物を確認し、買い取り金額をご提示します。 ③ ご納得いただけた場合は、現金をお渡しします。 出張買取 ① 最寄りの店舗にお電話ください。 ② 弊社スタッフがご自宅へ伺います。 ③ ご自宅で品物を確認し、買い取り金額をご提示します。 ④ ご納得いただけた場合は、現金をお渡しします。 宅配買取 ① ホームページからご予約ください。 ② 宅配業者が伺いますので、品物をお渡しください。 ③ 店舗への品物到着から３営業日以内に、買い取り金額をメールでご連絡します。ご納得いただけた場合は、銀行口座へお振り込みします。 ※買い取り金額にご納得いただけずキャンセルされる場合でも、出張料や返送料はかかりません。 ◆受け付け可能な品数とサイズ 受け付け可能な品数 １点あたりのサイズ制限 店頭買取 １点から なし 出張買取 ２点から なし 宅配買取 １点から 25kg以下で、かつ三辺（縦・横・高さ）の合計が160cm以下のもの（自転車の場合、サイズ内でも宅配買取は利用できません） ◆ご本人確認について 店頭買取、出張買取の場合、本人確認書類（運転免許証等）をご提示いただきます。宅配買取の場合は、品物と一緒にコピーをお送りいただきます。 家電、家具、楽器の場合は、顔写真のない本人確認書類もご利用になれますが、自転車の場合は、顔写真付きのものをご用意ください。
p_question-3932.en: Masuda Buyback Service Masuda Buyback Service Using Our Buyback Service ◆ Items We Can Buy We accept home appliances such as refrigerators, furniture such as desks, bicycles, and musical instruments. For details, please see the “Items We Can Buy” page. ◆ Methods and Process In-Store 1) Bring your items to the store. 2) We check the items and present the buyback price. 3) If you agree, we pay you in cash. Home Visit 1) Call your nearest store. 2) Our staff will visit your home. 3) We check the items at your home and present the buyback price. 4) If you agree, we pay you in cash. Courier 1) Make a reservation on our website. 2) A courier will come; hand over your items. 3) Within three business days after the items arrive at the store, we will email you the buyback price. If you agree, we transfer the funds to your bank account. ★ Even if you cancel because you are not satisfied with the price, there is no charge for the home visit or return shipping. ◆ Acceptable Quantity and Size Acceptable Quantity Size Limit per Item In-Store From 1 item No limit Home Visit From 2 items No limit Courier From 1 item Items up to 25 kg and with a combined length + width + height of 160 cm or less (for bicycles, courier buyback is not available even if within size limits) ◆ Identity Verification For in-store and home-visit buybacks, please present identification (driver’s license, etc.). For courier buybacks, please send a copy together with the item(s). For appliances, furniture, and instruments, ID without a photo may be used, but for bicycles, please prepare photo ID.
p_question-3932.ko: 마스다 매입 서비스 — 매입 서비스 이용 방법 ◆ 매입 가능 품목: 냉장고 등 가전, 책상 등 가구, 자전거, 악기 등. 자세한 건 "매입 가능 품목" 페이지 참조. ◆ 방법·흐름. [매장 방문] 1) 물건 지참 2) 검수 후 매입가 제시 3) 동의 시 현금 지급. [방문 매입] 1) 가까운 매장에 전화 2) 직원이 댁 방문 3) 댁에서 검수·매입가 제시 4) 동의 시 현금 지급. [택배] 1) 웹사이트 예약 2) 택배기사가 와서 인수 3) 매장 도착 후 영업일 3일 내 매입가를 메일 통보. 동의 시 계좌 이체. ★ 가격 미동의 취소 시 방문·반송 비용 무료. ◆ 가능 수량·크기. [매장 방문] 1점부터, 크기 제한 없음. [방문 매입] 2점부터, 크기 제한 없음. [택배] 1점부터, 25kg 이내 + 가로+세로+높이 합 160cm 이내(자전거는 사이즈 내라도 택배 매입 불가). ◆ 본인 확인. 매장·방문 매입 시 신분증(운전면허 등) 제시. 택배 매입 시 사본을 함께 송부. 가전·가구·악기는 사진 없는 신분증도 가능, 자전거는 사진 있는 신분증 필수.

questions.stem: ある画家の人生の軌跡を描いた映画を見た。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: きせき is correct. Question Translation "ある画家の人生の軌跡を描いた映画を見た。" → "I watched a movie depicting the path of a certain painter's life." Explanation The kanji "軌跡" is read as きせき, meaning *trajectory* or *path*, which accurately fits the context of depicting a life journey in the movie. Other Options 1. きぜき – Not a standard reading of 軌跡. 3. きゅうせき – Suggests *old trace*, but uses a different kanji (旧跡). 4. きゅうぜき – Not a standard reading and does not fit this context. Only きせき matches the kanji 軌跡 in this context.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (きせき). 한자 「軌跡」은 '궤적, 발자취'의 의미로 きせき로 읽으며, 한 화가의 인생 여정을 그린 영화라는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. 軌(궤도)와 跡(자취)가 결합된 단어로 인생의 흐름을 비유적으로 표현할 때 자주 쓰입니다. 오답: 1. きぜき는 표준 음독이 아닙니다. 3. きゅうせき는 旧跡(옛 자취)에 해당하는 다른 한자 표기로 軌跡과 무관합니다. 4. きゅうぜき 역시 軌跡의 표준 읽기가 아닙니다.

questions.stem: ここに書いてある情報には偏りがある。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: かたより is correct. Question Translation "ここに書いてある情報には偏りがある。" → "The information written here is biased." Explanation The kanji "偏り" is read as かたより, which means *bias* or *partiality*. This is the correct reading in the context of describing information that is not balanced. Other Options 1. へだたり – Means *gap* or *distance* (kanji: 隔たり), not appropriate here. 3. あやまり – Means *mistake* or *error* (kanji: 誤り), does not match 偏り. 4. こだわり – Means *obsession* or *fixation* (kanji: 拘り), and does not fit the context of 偏り. Only かたより matches the kanji 偏り in this context.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (かたより). 한자 「偏り」는 '치우침, 편향'을 뜻하며 정보가 균형을 잃어 한쪽으로 기울어 있다는 문맥에 어울리는 훈독입니다. 偏(치우칠 편)에서 온 명사형으로 정보·의견·시각 등의 편향을 나타낼 때 자주 사용됩니다. 오답: 1. へだたり는 隔たり(거리·격차)의 훈독입니다. 3. あやまり는 誤り(잘못)에 해당합니다. 4. こだわり는 拘り(집착)로 한자도 의미도 다른 단어입니다.

questions.stem: 村上むらかみ氏の主張にはさまざまな矛盾が含まれている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: むじゅん is correct. Question Translation "[村上|むらかみ]氏の主張にはさまざまな矛盾が含まれている。" → "Mr. Murakami's argument contains various contradictions." Explanation The kanji "矛盾" is read as むじゅん, which means *contradiction*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence. Other Options 2. むじょう – Suggests *without emotion* (kanji: 無情), and does not match 矛盾. 3. みじゅん – Not a standard reading of 矛盾. 4. みじょう – Not a standard reading of 矛盾. Only むじゅん matches the kanji 矛盾 in this context.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (むじゅん). 한자 「矛盾」은 '모순'으로 むじゅん이 정확한 음독이며, 무라카미 씨의 주장에 여러 모순이 포함되어 있다는 문맥에 부합합니다. 矛(창)와 盾(방패) 고사에서 유래한 말로 논리적으로 양립할 수 없는 두 진술을 가리킵니다. 오답: 2. むじょう는 無情(무정)이라는 다른 단어. 3. みじゅん과 4. みじょう는 모두 矛盾의 표준 읽기가 아니며 의미도 통하지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 事実が誇張されて伝わる。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: こちょう is correct. Question Translation "事実が誇張されて伝わる。" → "The facts are conveyed in an exaggerated manner." Explanation The kanji "誇張" is read as こちょう, which means *exaggeration*. This reading is appropriate in the context of the sentence, which discusses how facts are communicated. Other Options 1. こうちょう – Suggests a different kanji, such as *校長* for principal. 2. ごちょう – Not a standard reading for 誇張. 4. ごうちょう – Not a standard reading for 誇張. Only こちょう fits the kanji 誇張 in this context.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (こちょう). 한자 「誇張」은 '과장'을 뜻하며 こちょう로 읽고, 사실이 과장되어 전해진다는 문맥에 자연스럽게 들어맞습니다. 誇(자랑할 과)와 張(베풀 장)이 결합되어 사실 이상으로 부풀려 표현하는 행위를 의미합니다. 오답: 1. こうちょう는 校長(교장)·好調(호조) 등 다른 한자에 해당합니다. 2. ごちょう, 4. ごうちょう는 誇張의 표준 음독이 아닙니다.

questions.stem: この食堂で社員全員の昼食を賄っている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: まかなって is correct. Question Translation "この食堂で社員全員の昼食を賄っている。" → "This cafeteria provides lunch for all employees." Explanation The kanji "賄って" is read as まかなって, which means *to provide* or *to cover (expenses)*. This reading fits the context of the sentence, where the cafeteria is providing lunch. Other Options 1. ふるまって – Means *to serve* or *to behave* (kanji: 振る舞って), but not a match here. 2. になって – Means *to become* or *to act as* (kanji: になって), unrelated to providing. 3. あつかって – Means *to handle* or *to deal with* (kanji: 扱って), not suitable for providing a service. Only まかなって matches the context of 賄って in this sentence.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (まかなって). 한자 「賄って」는 '(비용·식사 등을) 충당하다, 마련하다'의 의미로 まかなって가 맞고, 사원 전체의 점심을 식당이 제공한다는 문맥에 부합합니다. 賄う는 정해진 자원·예산 안에서 필요를 메운다는 뉘앙스를 가집니다. 오답: 1. ふるまって는 振る舞う(행동하다·대접하다). 2. になって는 担う(짊어지다). 3. あつかって는 扱う(다루다)로 모두 한자가 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 軽率な行動が批判を招いた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: けいそつ is correct. Question Translation "軽率な行動が批判を招いた。" → "Reckless actions invited criticism." Explanation The kanji "軽率" is read as けいそつ, which means *reckless* or *careless*. This is the appropriate reading for the kanji in this context, referring to behavior that leads to criticism. Other Options 2. けいりつ – Not related to 軽率. 3. かいそつ – Does not correspond to any typical reading or meaning. 4. かいりつ – Means *regulation* or *rule* (kanji: 戒律), unrelated to 軽率. Only けいそつ fits both the kanji 軽率 and the context of the sentence.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (けいそつ). 한자 「軽率」은 '경솔'의 의미로 けいそつ로 읽으며, 경솔한 행동이 비판을 불러왔다는 문맥에 정확히 맞습니다. 軽(가벼울 경)과 率(거느릴 솔)이 결합된 한자어로 깊이 생각하지 않고 행동하는 태도를 가리킵니다. 오답: 2. けいりつ는 軽率의 표준 읽기가 아닙니다. 3. かいそつ도 해당 음독이 없습니다. 4. かいりつ는 戒律(계율)에 해당하는 전혀 다른 한자입니다.

questions.stem: そのチームは名門チームが多数出場する中で初出場で優勝という（ ）を成し遂げた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 快挙 (かいきょ) is correct. It means "a splendid achievement" or "a remarkable feat." Question Translation The sentence translates to: "In the midst of many prestigious teams participating, that team achieved the remarkable feat of winning on their first appearance." Explanation The sentence highlights an extraordinary accomplishment by stating that the team won the championship during their debut amidst strong competition. 快挙 (かいきょ) precisely captures the sense of an impressive or remarkable achievement, which aligns well with the context of successfully winning against significant odds. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 好況 (こうきょう) means "prosperity" or "good economic conditions." It refers to economic or business contexts, not fitting for describing a sports team’s achievement. - Answer 2: 喜劇 (きげき) means "comedy." It refers to a genre of entertainment, not applicable to the context of a sports victory. - Answer 4: 繁栄 (はんえい) means "prosperity" or "thriving," typically used to describe economies, businesses, or societies prospering over time. It doesn’t relate to the context of a sports achievement. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The term 快挙 (かいきょ) is often used in contexts where a notable and commendable achievement is highlighted, such as in sports, academics, or any field where accomplishing something remarkable is notable. Recognizing this term is helpful in understanding contexts of notable achievements or successes.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (快挙). '쾌거'를 뜻하며, 명문 팀들이 다수 출전한 가운데 첫 출전 우승이라는 놀라운 성과를 이뤄냈다는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. 快挙는 비범하고 칭송할 만한 업적을 가리키는 표현입니다. 오답: 1. 好況은 '호황'으로 경기 상황을 가리킵니다. 2. 喜劇는 '희극'(연극 장르). 4. 繁栄는 '번영'(국가·산업의 번성)으로 모두 개별 사건의 일회적 성취를 나타내지 못합니다.

questions.stem: リーダーの無責任な発言が、混乱をさらに（ ）することになった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 助長 (じょちょう) is correct. It means "to promote" or "to encourage," often in a context where something undesirable is being exacerbated. Question Translation The leader's irresponsible statement ended up further promoting the confusion. Explanation The sentence describes how the leader's careless words made an existing problem worse, specifically confusion. The term 助長 is used when actions unintentionally encourage or exacerbate a negative situation. In this context, the leader’s statement is contributing to or worsening the confusion. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 拡充 (かくじゅう) means "expansion" or "enlargement." This term is generally used in a positive or neutral context, such as expanding a service or facility, and doesn’t fit the negative context of worsening confusion. - Answer 3: 主導 (しゅどう) means "leadership" or "initiative." It implies taking a leading role or directing something, which does not align with the context of causing additional confusion. - Answer 4: 催促 (さいそく) means "urging" or "pressing." It refers to prompting someone to do something more quickly, which doesn’t fit the context of the sentence where confusion is being promoted or exacerbated. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Understanding the nuance of 助長 is key here, as it conveys the idea of unintentionally worsening a negative situation. Recognizing when certain actions lead to negative consequences, even when not intended, is vital for understanding the contextually-defined expre
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (助長). '조장'으로, 리더의 무책임한 발언이 혼란을 더 부추겼다는 문맥에 부합하며 부정적 상황의 악화를 나타낼 때 전형적으로 쓰이는 단어입니다. 의도와 무관하게 좋지 않은 사태를 키운다는 뉘앙스를 가집니다. 오답: 2. 拡充은 '확충'으로 시설·내용 확대에 사용. 3. 主導는 '주도'로 적극적으로 이끎. 4. 催促은 '재촉'으로 의미가 모두 맞지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 誰かを助けてあげたからといって、その分の（ ）を求めてはいけない。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 見返り is correct. It means "return" or "compensation." Question Translation Just because you helped someone, you shouldn't seek compensation for it. Explanation The sentence discusses the act of helping someone and suggests that one should not expect something in return for their help. 見返り (みかえり) refers to a return benefit or compensation one might expect after doing a favor, which perfectly fits this context where it's emphasized that such expectation should be avoided. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 仕返し（しかえし） means "retaliation" or "revenge." This doesn't fit the context, as it implies responding to a negative act or harm, rather than expecting something positive in return for a good deed. - Answer 3: 返却（へんきゃく） means "return" in the sense of returning something that was borrowed or rented. It is not appropriate here because it specifically involves the act of returning a borrowed item, not expecting compensation for a favor. - Answer 4: 返済（へんさい） means "repayment," typically involving money or debt. It's unsuitable because the context is not about financial repayment but rather about an expectation of a reciprocal act or benefit. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The expression "(誰かを助けてあげたからといって)" uses the construction 'からといって,' which is an idiomatic expression that conveys caution against assuming that a certain action automatically justifies another, emphasizing the moral lesson of not expecting anything in return for an altruistic action. Recogni
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (見返り). '대가, 보답'의 의미로, 누군가를 도왔다고 해서 그에 대한 보상을 바라서는 안 된다는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. 호의에 대한 반대급부를 가리키는 일상 표현입니다. 오답: 1. 仕返し는 '보복·복수'로 부정적 갚음. 3. 返却는 '반환'으로 빌린 것을 돌려주는 행위. 4. 返済는 '변제'로 빚을 갚는 행위로 모두 호의에 대한 보답과는 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 学内から選ばれた学生たちによって、代表チームが（ ）された。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 結成 (けっせい) is correct. It means "formation" or "establishment." Question Translation A representative team was formed by the students selected from within the school. Explanation The sentence talks about creating or forming a representative team. 結成 (けっせい) means "formation," and it is commonly used to describe the act of forming a team, group, or organization. This word precisely describes the process of assembling a team from selected students. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 集約 (しゅうやく) means "consolidation" or "integration," often used in contexts like summarizing or centralizing information. It doesn’t fit as it doesn’t describe creating a team. - Answer 2: 結合 (けつごう) means "combination" or "union." While this involves joining, it doesn’t specifically imply the organized formation of a team or group, making 結成 more appropriate. - Answer 4: 採集 (さいしゅう) means "collection" or "gathering," often used in contexts involving collecting items or data. It isn’t used for forming teams or groups. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The term 結成 is often seen in contexts where a group is officially organized or established. It is important for JLPT test-takers to recognize common collocations and how certain verbs specifically relate to assembling or forming groups in Japanese.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (結成). '결성'으로, 학내에서 선발된 학생들에 의해 대표 팀이 만들어졌다는 문맥에 자연스럽게 어울립니다. 팀·그룹·조직 등을 새로 조직하는 행위를 표현하는 정형 어휘입니다. 오답: 1. 集約는 '집약'으로 의견·정보의 통합. 2. 結合는 '결합'으로 물리적·화학적 결합. 4. 採集는 '채집'으로 표본·샘플 수집을 가리키므로 모두 팀 결성과는 부적합합니다.

questions.stem: 飛行機やホテルの予約、関係者との日程調整など、出張の（ ）はすべて秘書に任せている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 手配 (てはい) is correct. It means "arrangement" or "preparation." Question Translation For things like flight and hotel reservations, scheduling with stakeholders, etc., all arrangements for business trips are entrusted to the secretary. Explanation The context describes various activities related to organizing a business trip, such as booking flights and hotels or coordinating schedules. The word 手配 (てはい) refers to the process of arranging or organizing these logistics, which fits perfectly with the activities mentioned in the sentence. It is common to use 手配 when talking about organizing and managing practical aspects of events or travel. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 始末 (しまつ) means "handling a situation" or "settlement," often in the context of resolving problems or dealing with something undesirable. It is not appropriate here, as the sentence is detailing regular organizational tasks, not problem-solving. - Answer 3: 実施 (じっし) means "execution" or "implementation." This would imply actively carrying out tasks or projects rather than arranging them. It doesn’t fit the context in which the secretary is arranging logistics. - Answer 4: 整備 (せいび) means "maintenance" or "servicing," typically related to keeping equipment or systems in good condition. It doesn’t apply to scheduling or making reservations. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The word 手配 (てはい) is often used in contexts involving planning or coordinating logistics, such as travel arrangements
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (手配). '수배·준비'의 의미로 비행기·호텔 예약, 일정 조율 등 출장과 관련된 모든 준비 활동을 비서에게 맡긴다는 문맥 전반을 포괄하기에 가장 적절합니다. 手配는 사람이 처리해야 할 사무·물자의 사전 준비를 가리킵니다. 오답: 2. 始末는 '뒤처리'(사후 정리). 3. 実施는 '실시'(실행). 4. 整備는 '정비'(설비·서류 정돈)로 출장 준비 전반에는 어울리지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 入院生活をして、健康の大切さを（ ）感じた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: つくづく is correct. It means "deeply" or "keenly." Question Translation Through my hospital stay, I deeply felt the importance of health. Explanation The context of the sentence involves someone reflecting on the significance of health during a hospital stay. "つくづく感じた" means to feel or realize something deeply or thoroughly, which aligns perfectly with the context of gaining a profound appreciation or understanding of something important, like health, during a significant experience such as hospitalization. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: めきめき means "remarkably" or "rapidly," often used to describe visible or noticeable progress or improvement, such as in abilities or skills. It doesn't fit the context of feeling or realizing something deeply. - Answer 2: せいぜい means "at most" or "at best," typically used in contexts expressing limitation or constraint. It doesn't relate to the depth of realization or feeling required in the sentence. - Answer 3: はきはき means "clearly" or "briskly," often describing the manner of speaking or a person's behavior as clear and decisive. It's not suitable for describing an emotion or feeling deeply. Grammar/Vocabulary Note つくづく (deeply) is often used to express a realization or reflection that has a significant impact, often gained through personal experience or contemplation. Recognizing the subtle nuances of words expressing mental states or reflections is crucial in accurately interpreting Japanese contextually-d
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (つくづく). '절실히, 깊이'의 의미로, 입원 생활을 통해 건강의 소중함을 깊이 느꼈다는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. つくづく感じる는 어떤 사실을 곱씹어 통감하는 정형 표현입니다. 오답: 1. めきめき는 '눈에 띄게'로 성장·향상에 사용. 2. せいぜい는 '기껏해야'로 한도. 3. はきはき는 '시원시원하게'로 말투 묘사이므로 모두 의미가 맞지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 高木たかぎさんは入社当初は表情が硬かったが、だんだん緊張が（ ）きたらしく、笑顔を見せるようになった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: ほぐれて is correct. It means "to unwind," "to relax," or "to become loosened." Question Translation When Mr. Takagi first joined the company, his expression was stiff, but as he gradually seemed to relax, he started showing smiles. Explanation The sentence describes a change in Mr. Takagi’s demeanor over time. Initially, he appeared tense, but gradually his tension eased, allowing him to smile more. The word ほぐれて (ほぐれる) is used to express the relaxing or easing of tension, which makes it the best fit contextually. In this scenario, it describes the process of becoming more relaxed or at ease, which matches the progression from stiffness to the ability to smile. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: かすれて (かすれる) means "to become blurry" or "to fade," usually concerning sounds or images. It doesn’t apply to emotions or expressions becoming relaxed. - Answer 2: はがれて (はがれる) means "to peel off" or "to come unstuck," which is physical action and doesn’t match the context of describing emotional states. - Answer 3: くずれて (くずれる) means "to crumble" or "to collapse," often used in physical or structural contexts rather than describing relaxation or easing of emotions. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Understanding the nuances of words describing changes in state or condition is vital. Words like ほぐれる, which indicate relaxation, represent a shift from a tense state, fitting emotional and situational contexts particularly in this sentence structure. Recognizing these con
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (ほぐれて). 「ほぐれる」는 '풀리다, 누그러지다'의 의미로 입사 초기의 긴장이 점차 풀려 미소를 짓게 되었다는 문맥에 가장 잘 맞습니다. 긴장·근육·매듭 등이 풀리는 상태를 자연스럽게 표현합니다. 오답: 1. かすれて는 '갈라지다·잠기다'(목소리). 2. はがれて는 '벗겨지다'(표면). 3. くずれて는 '무너지다'로 긴장 완화의 자연스러운 표현은 ほぐれる입니다.

questions.stem: 自分の尺度を持ったほうがいい。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 基準 is correct. It means "standard" or "criterion." Question Translation It is better to have your own criteria. Explanation The sentence implies that having your own way to judge or evaluate things is important. 尺度 (しゃくど) means "measure" or "criterion," which aligns well with 基準 (きじゅん), meaning "standard" or "criterion." Both terms are used to describe a basis for evaluation or judgment. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 理想 (りそう) means "ideal." While it might seem like a positive way to guide one's actions, it doesn't carry the same practical evaluative aspect as having a standard or criterion. - Answer 2: 意思 (いし) means "will" or "intention." This doesn't fit because the sentence is about having a way to evaluate or measure, not about one's intentions or desires. - Answer 4: 専門 (せんもん) means "specialty" or "expertise." This term is more about specific skills or knowledge areas rather than general standards or criteria for judgment. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Recognizing terms like 尺度 (しゃくど), which are associated with measurement and evaluation, can help identify correct paraphrases like 基準 (きじゅん). Understanding nuances in meaning between words such as 理想 and 基準 is crucial for selecting the most appropriate word in context.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (基準). 「尺度」(잣대)와 가장 가까운 의미는 '기준'으로, 자신만의 판단 기준을 가지는 편이 좋다는 의미에 부합합니다. 尺度도 基準도 평가·판단의 토대를 가리킨다는 점에서 동의어 관계입니다. 오답: 1. 理想은 '이상'으로 추구하는 모습. 2. 意思는 '의사'로 의지·결심. 4. 専門은 '전문 분야'를 의미하므로 평가 척도라는 의미와는 모두 거리가 있습니다.

questions.stem: わずらわしい仕事をやることになってしまった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 面倒な is correct. It means "troublesome" or "bothersome." Question Translation "I ended up having to do a troublesome job." Explanation The key word here is わずらわしい, which means "troublesome" or "annoying." This kind of task typically requires more effort, attention, or time than is desired, creating inconvenience or difficulty. 面倒な (めんどうな) directly conveys this sense of a task being a hassle or a burden, making it the perfect paraphrase. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 退屈な (たいくつな) means "boring" or "dull." This word refers to something that lacks excitement or interest, rather than something that's troublesome or difficult. - Answer 3: 危険な (きけんな) means "dangerous." It implies a risk of harm, which is not the same as being troublesome or inconvenient. - Answer 4: 余計な (よけいな) means "unnecessary" or "needless." While it suggests something is not needed, it doesn't convey the sense of annoyance or bother that わずらわしい does. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Understanding nuances in adjectives is crucial for this type of question. The word わずらわしい, like 面倒な (めんどうな), conveys a specific type of inconvenience or annoyance. Recognizing synonyms and their context helps choose the most precise paraphrase.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (面倒な). 「わずらわしい」는 '귀찮은, 성가신'의 뜻으로, 시간과 수고가 많이 드는 일을 가리키며 「面倒な」와 의미가 정확히 일치합니다. 둘 다 부담스럽고 처리가 번거로운 일에 대한 부정적 감정을 표현합니다. 오답: 1. 退屈な는 '지루한'(흥미 부족). 3. 危険な는 '위험한'(안전 위협). 4. 余計な는 '쓸데없는'(불필요)으로 '번거로움'의 핵심 뉘앙스와는 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 池田いけださんには会えたが、肝心な話はできなかった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 重要な is correct. It means "important." Question Translation I was able to meet Ms. Ikeda, but I couldn't have the important conversation. Explanation The sentence implies there was a specific conversation intended to be had with Ms. Ikeda that was essential or of high importance. The word "肝心な" (かんじんな) translates to "crucial" or "important," making "重要な" (じゅうような) the most suitable paraphrase because it captures the essential nature of the topic that could not be discussed. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 個人的な (こじんてきな) means "personal." While a conversation could be personal, this does not inherently mean it is crucial or important, which is why it doesn’t capture the meaning of "肝心な." - Answer 2: 具体的な (ぐたいてきな) means "specific" or "concrete." This choice refers to the detailed or defined nature of a conversation rather than its importance, which is the key aspect needed here. - Answer 3: 詳細な (しょうさいな) means "detailed." While a conversation could be detailed, this quality does not relate to its importance, thus failing to paraphrase "肝心な" correctly. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Both "肝心な" and "重要な" convey the notion of significance or importance. Recognizing the similarity between these terms is crucial for effective paraphrasing. Understanding the subtle differences and appropriate contexts for usage of such words is vital for the JLPT paraphrase questions.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (重要な). 「肝心な」는 '핵심적인, 매우 중요한'의 의미로 「重要な」와 같은 뜻이며, 이케다 씨를 만나긴 했지만 정작 중요한 이야기를 못 했다는 문맥에 부합합니다. 肝(간)과 心(심장)이라는 핵심 장기에서 유래한 비유 표현입니다. 오답: 1. 個人的な는 '개인적인'. 2. 具体的な는 '구체적인'. 3. 詳細な는 '상세한'으로 '핵심·중요'의 직접적 동의어가 아닙니다.

questions.stem: 今週は仕事がはかどりました。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 順調に進みました is correct. It means "progressed smoothly." Question Translation This week, work progressed steadily. Explanation In the sentence, "はかどりました" (捗りました) suggests that work has been advancing well or efficiently. The correct paraphrase is "順調に進みました," which translates to "progressed smoothly" or "went well," perfectly capturing the essence of “はかどりました,” implying effective and seamless progress in work tasks. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 大幅に遅れました means "was significantly delayed." This is the opposite of the intended meaning. "遅れました" indicates a lack of progress or falling behind, which contradicts the sense of successful advancement. - Answer 3: 徐々に減りました means "gradually decreased." This doesn’t relate to progress in work and suggests a reduction, which isn’t applicable in the context of how work was accomplished. - Answer 4: 急激に増えました means "increased rapidly." While this describes a change in quantity or intensity, it doesn’t convey the smooth, efficient progression of work described by "はかどりました." Grammar/Vocabulary Note The verb 捗る (はかどる) is used to express that some task is going well or advancing effortlessly. It's a term used in contexts where efficiency in completing a task or project is discussed, highlighting the successful progression without obstacles. Recognizing this term helps in distinguishing it from actions indicating delay or change in level/intensity unrelated to progress efficiency.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (順調に進みました). 「はかどる(捗る)」는 '일이 순조롭게 진척되다'의 뜻으로, '순조롭게 진행되었다'와 동일한 의미입니다. 작업이 예정대로 효율적으로 나아갔다는 긍정적 평가를 나타냅니다. 오답: 2. 大幅に遅れました는 '크게 지연되었다'로 정반대 의미. 3. 徐々に減りました는 '서서히 줄었다'(양적 감소). 4. 急激に増えました는 '급증했다'로 모두 진척의 의미와 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 新人のときはずっと辛抱していた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 我慢 is correct. It means "endurance" or "patience." Question Translation When I was a newcomer, I patiently endured the entire time. Explanation The word 辛抱 (しんぼう) closely aligns with 我慢 (がまん), both expressing the concept of enduring a difficult situation with patience and perseverance. In the context of being new at a job or in a new environment, 我慢 accurately reflects the notion of enduring challenges or difficulties without complaint, which is why it is the best fit here. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 苦労 (くろう) means "hardship" or "trouble." While it also relates to enduring difficulties, it focuses more on the difficulties themselves rather than the act of enduring them. - Answer 3: 失敗 (しっぱい) means "failure." This does not fit the context, as the sentence is about enduring with patience rather than experiencing failure. - Answer 4: 心配 (しんぱい) means "worry" or "concern." This term doesn’t align with the concept of endurance or patience; rather, it refers to having worries or being anxious. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 辛抱 (しんぼう) and 我慢 (がまん) are often used interchangeably, but 辛抱 may stress more on enduring broader life situations, while 我慢 emphasizes holding one's patience in specific situations. Recognizing such nuances can help in understanding the context and choosing the appropriate synonym.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (我慢). 「辛抱」와 「我慢」은 모두 '참고 견딤, 인내'를 뜻하는 동의어로, 신입 시절 내내 인내했다는 문맥에 가장 자연스럽게 일치합니다. 어려운 상황을 불평 없이 버텨내는 태도를 나타냅니다. 오답: 1. 苦労는 '고생'으로 어려움 자체. 3. 失敗는 '실패'로 결과. 4. 心配는 '걱정'으로 불안 감정이라 인내라는 직접적 동의어가 되지 못합니다.

questions.stem: この地方のしきたりについて話を聞いた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 慣習 is correct. It means "custom" or "tradition." Question Translation I heard a story about the customs of this region. Explanation The sentence involves a discussion or story about the local cultural practices of an area. The word "しきたり" (shikitari) means "customs" or "traditions," and the closest word that matches this meaning from the options provided is 慣習 (かんしゅう), which refers to the traditional customs or usual practices of a place or people. This makes Answer 3 the best fit for the sentence. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 名物 (めいぶつ) means "specialty" or "notable product." It usually refers to famous products or specialties of a region, not necessarily the customs or traditions. - Answer 2: 気候 (きこう) means "climate." While climate can influence customs, this option directly refers to the weather conditions of a place and not the practices or traditions. - Answer 4: 歴史 (れきし) means "history." Although history can include customs, it broadly encompasses all historical events and developments, not specifically the current customs or traditions. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The word 慣習 (かんしゅう) is important in discussions about cultural practices and societal norms. Recognizing synonym patterns and understanding subtle distinctions between words like customs, climate, and history are key in comprehending the nuance in paraphrases for the JLPT.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (慣習). 「しきたり」는 '관습, 풍습'의 의미로 「慣習」과 정확히 일치하며, 지방의 전통적 관행에 관해 들었다는 문맥에 부합합니다. 둘 다 한 지역·집단에서 오래도록 이어져 온 관행적 행동 양식을 가리킵니다. 오답: 1. 名物은 '명물·특산'. 2. 気候는 '기후'(자연 환경). 4. 歴史는 '역사'(시간적 흐름)이라 지역 관습의 직접 동의어로 적절하지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 解約
opts: 道路工事が終了したので、車両の通行止めを解約した。
暗証番号を入力して、ドアのロックを解約してください。
体調が悪いので、友達と食事に行く予定を解約した。
家計の見直しをして、保険を解約することにした。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4 is correct. Word Translation 解約（かいやく） — cancellation (of a contract or subscription) Correct Sentence Translation I reviewed my household budget and decided to cancel my insurance. Explanation The word 解約 is used specifically for canceling formal agreements, contracts, or subscriptions. "保険を解約する" (to cancel insurance) is a prototypical and correct use of this word, as insurance policies are formal agreements. Other Options ① 道路工事が終了したので、車両の通行止めを__解約__した。 → The road construction finished, so the vehicle traffic ban was canceled. Incorrect Usage "解約" is unsuitable here. Instead, "解除" (かいじょ, to lift/release) would be appropriate for restrictions or bans. ② 暗証番号を入力して、ドアのロックを__解約__してください。 → Enter the PIN and cancel the door lock. Incorrect Usage "解約" does not apply to unlocking or deactivating something briefly. Use "解除" or "開錠" (かいじょう, to unlock) instead. ③ 体調が悪いので、友達と食事に行く予定を__解約__した。 → I felt unwell, so I canceled plans to eat with friends. Incorrect Usage For informal plans, "キャンセル" (cancel) or "取り消した" (canceled) would be appropriate, as "解約" is overly formal. Usage Note "解約" is typically reserved for formal agreements, such as contracts, subscriptions, or policies. It conveys a sense of legally or formally terminating an arrangement, rather than simply canceling a casual plan or action.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (가계 점검 후 보험을 解約하기로 함). 「解約」은 계약·계좌·보험 등 정식 계약 관계의 해지에 사용되는 용어이며 '保険を解約する'는 가장 전형적인 용법입니다. 오답: 1. 도로 통행 금지 해제는 '解除'가 적절. 2. 도어 록 해제 역시 '解除'. 3. 친구와의 식사 약속 취소는 'キャンセル'나 '取り消し'를 사용해야 자연스러우며, 이런 일상적 약속에는 解約를 쓰지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 特産
opts: これはある監督の数々の作品の中でも特産の映画だと思う。
村の特産の黄色いりんごを使った料理をごちそうになった。
彼はわが国の特産の世界的なサッカー選手で、彼のことを知らない人はいない。
わが社特産の技術を生かした新商品を開発する。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2 is correct. Word Translation 特産（とくさん） — local product, specialty, special product of a region Correct Sentence Translation I was treated to a dish made with yellow apples, the specialty of the village. Explanation The word 特産 refers to products that are uniquely associated with a particular region, often used in the context of foods or goods. "村の特産の黄色いりんご" (the village's specialty yellow apples) is a natural expression that highlights a local specialty. Other Options ① これはある監督の数々の作品の中でも__特産__の映画だと思う。 → Among the many works of this director, I think this film is the specialty. Incorrect Usage "特産" does not refer to movies or artistic works. More appropriate words might be "代表作" (representative work) or "傑作" (masterpiece). ③ 彼はわが国の__特産__の世界的なサッカー選手で、彼のことを知らない人はいない。 → He is a world-renowned soccer player, a specialty of our country, and everyone knows him. Incorrect Usage People, especially athletes, are not described as "特産". A suitable term could be "スター選手" (star player) or "有名な人物" (famous person). ④ わが社__特産__の技術を生かした新商品を開発する。 → We are developing a new product using our company's specialty technology. Misleading Usage While "特産" is not typical for a company's technology, it might be more acceptable in rare marketing contexts. Nevertheless, "得意の" (specialty) or "独自の" (unique) would be preferable. Usage Note "特産" is best used with tangible products that are locally or regionally notable. It often relates to agricultural goods, artisanal items, or other re
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 2번 (마을의 特産인 노란 사과 요리를 대접받음). 「特産」은 특정 지역에서만 생산되는 특산물을 가리키므로 '村の特産の黄色いりんご'는 자연스러운 표현입니다. 오답: 1. 영화 작품 평가에는 '特産'이 아닌 '特別な' 등이 적절. 3. 사람(축구 선수)에게는 '特産'을 쓸 수 없으며 '誇る' 등이 적합. 4. 회사의 기술에는 '独自の' 같은 표현이 자연스럽고 特産은 부적합합니다.

questions.stem: 問い詰める
opts: 石川いしかわさんは数学ができるから、いつもみんなに数学の問題を問い詰められている。
進路について一人で問い詰めていたが、両親に相談することにした。
子どものころから星を見るのが大好きで、今も宇宙の謎を問い詰めている。
友人たちから、どうして約束を破ったのかと問い詰められた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4 is correct. Word Translation 問い詰める（といつめる） — to interrogate, to press someone for an answer Correct Sentence Translation My friends pressed me for an answer, asking why I broke the promise. Explanation The word 問い詰める involves pressing someone for a clear answer, often in a confrontational or accusatory manner. "約束を破ったのか" (why I broke the promise) fits naturally as it implies a direct confrontation where someone demands an explanation. Other Options ① 石川さんは数学ができるから、いつもみんなに数学の問題を__問い詰められて__いる。 → Ishikawa-san is good at math, so everyone always interrogates him about math problems. Incorrect Usage "問い詰める" is too strong for describing casually asking about math problems. A more fitting verb might be "質問される" (be asked questions). ② 進路について一人で__問い詰めて__いたが、両親に相談することにした。 → I was interrogating myself about my future path, but decided to consult my parents. Awkward Usage "問い詰める" usually involves questioning others, not oneself. "考えていた" or "悩んでいた" (was thinking/concerned) would be more appropriate. ③ 子どものころから星を見るのが大好きで、今も宇宙の謎を__問い詰めて__いる。 → Since childhood, I've loved star-gazing and still interrogate the mysteries of the universe. Incorrect Usage "問い詰める" is inappropriate for exploring or investigating concepts. Better verbs include "追求している" (pursuing) or "探求している" (investigating). Usage Note "問い詰める" is typically employed in contexts where a person is being pressured to provide a justification or reveal details, often with a critical or demanding tone. It is not sui
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 4번 (친구들에게 약속을 어긴 이유를 問い詰められた). 「問い詰める」는 답을 끈질기게 추궁한다는 뜻으로, 약속 위반에 대한 추궁이라는 직접 대면 상황의 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. 오답: 1. 수학 문제는 '質問する'가 적절(추궁이 아님). 2. 진로 고민은 '悩む'가 자연스럽고 자기 자신에게 問い詰める는 어색. 3. 우주의 수수께끼 같은 학문적 대상은 '探究する'가 어울립니다.

questions.stem: 改修
opts: 顔の大きさに合わせて、眼鏡を改修してもらった。
問題の解決には、法律を改修しなければならない。
台風によって壊れてしまった橋を改修している。
この資料は古いので、データを改修してください。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3 is correct. Word Translation 改修（かいしゅう） — repair, renovation, improvement Correct Sentence Translation They are repairing the bridge that was destroyed by the typhoon. Explanation The word 改修 is used to describe physical repairs or renovations. In this context, "橋を改修している" (repairing the bridge) is a natural expression because it refers to fixing or improving a damaged structure. Other Options ① 顔の大きさに合わせて、眼鏡を__改修__してもらった。 → I had the glasses adjusted to fit the size of my face. Incorrect Usage "改修" is related to large-scale physical repairs, not minor adjustments like customizing glasses. The appropriate word would be "調整" (adjustment). ② 問題の解決には、法律を__改修__しなければならない。 → To solve the problem, the law needs to be revised. Awkward Usage While "改修" can imply changes, legal modifications are more accurately described as "改正" (revision). ④ この資料は古いので、データを__改修__してください。 → Please update the data because these materials are outdated. Incorrect Usage "改修" is not suitable for data updates. The proper term for revising or updating documents or data is "更新" (update) or "修正" (correction). Usage Note "改修" is primarily used in contexts involving structural repairs or improvements, such as buildings, roads, or infrastructure. It does not apply well to smaller scale adjustments, legal revisions, or informational updates.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (태풍으로 부서진 다리를 改修하고 있다). 「改修」는 건물·다리·시설 등 구조물의 본격적인 수리·개축에 쓰이며 다리 보수라는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. 오답: 1. 안경 크기 맞춤은 '調整'(미세 조정). 2. 법률에는 '改正'(법 개정)이 표준 표현. 4. 자료 데이터 갱신은 '更新'이 적절합니다. 改修는 작은 조정이나 추상적 갱신에는 사용하지 않습니다.

questions.stem: 手厚い
opts: 大切なお客様がいらっしゃったので、手厚くもてなした。
交差点で起きた交通事故の状況を警察に手厚く説明した。
この商品は壊れやすいので、袋に包んで手厚く運んだ。
寝ている赤ちゃんを起こさないように、ドアを手厚く閉めた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1 is correct. Word Translation 手厚い（てあつい） — courteous, hospitable, attentive Correct Sentence Translation Since an important guest came, we hosted them attentively. Explanation The word 手厚い is used to describe providing considerate and attentive care or hospitality. "手厚くもてなした" (hosted attentively) is a common and natural expression in Japanese, often used in contexts involving hospitality or care. Other Options ② 交差点で起きた交通事故の状況を警察に__手厚く__説明した。 → I explained the traffic accident situation at the intersection attentively to the police. Awkward Usage "手厚く説明した" is uncommon. For explanations, words like "詳しく" (in detail) or "丁寧に" (politely) fit better. ③ この商品は壊れやすいので、袋に包んで__手厚く__運んだ。 → This product is fragile, so I wrapped it in a bag and handled it attentively. Incorrect Usage "手厚く運んだ" is unnatural for handling objects. Use "慎重に" (carefully) or "丁寧に" (gently). ④ 寝ている赤ちゃんを起こさないように、ドアを__手厚く__閉めた。 → I closed the door attentively so as not to wake the sleeping baby. Incorrect Usage "手厚く閉めた" is odd for physical actions like closing doors. "静かに" (quietly) or "そっと" (gently) are more suitable. Usage Note "手厚い" typically emphasizes the thoroughness and care in interpersonal contexts, like hospitality or nursing. It doesn’t usually apply to explaining, handling objects, or gentle physical actions like closing a door.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (중요한 손님이 와서 手厚くもてなした). 「手厚い」는 '극진한, 정중한, 세심한'의 의미로 손님 접대에 쓰이는 정형 표현입니다. 'もてなす(대접하다)'와 결합해 '극진히 대접하다'라는 자연스러운 호응을 이룹니다. 오답: 2. 사고 상황 설명은 '丁寧に'가 적절. 3. 깨지기 쉬운 상품 운반은 '慎重に'. 4. 자는 아기를 깨우지 않게 문 닫기는 '静かに'가 자연스럽습니다.

questions.stem: デマ
opts: 誰かがデマを流して、私たちをだまそうとしている。
この絵画はデマで、本物は市の美術館で見られます。
山本さんは年上だと思っていたが、私のデマだった。
二人できちんと話し合って、お互いのデマを解いた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1 is correct. Word Translation デマ — false rumor, misinformation Correct Sentence Translation Someone is spreading a false rumor to deceive us. Explanation The word デマ is used for misinformation or false rumors that are spread, often intentionally to deceive others. "デマを流す" (to spread a false rumor) is a common and natural expression in Japanese. Other Options ② この絵画は__デマ__で、本物は市の美術館で見られます。 → This painting is misinformation, the real one is in the city museum. Incorrect Usage "デマ" does not describe physical objects like paintings. Instead, you'd use "偽物" (fake) or "レプリカ" (replica). ③ 山本さんは年上だと思っていたが、私の__デマ__だった。 → I thought Mr. Yamamoto was older, but it was my false rumor. Awkward Usage "デマ" usually implies spreading to deceive others, not personal misunderstanding. A more appropriate term would be "思い違い" (misunderstanding). ④ 二人できちんと話し合って、お互いの__デマ__を解いた。 → The two of us talked properly and resolved each other's false rumors. Incorrect Usage While resolving misunderstandings could be possible, "デマ" usually refers to harmful rumors spread beyond personal exchanges. "誤解" (misunderstanding) fits better here. Usage Note "デマ" is specifically used for false information or rumors intended to deceive a broader audience rather than personal misunderstandings. It is about misinformation being spread to mislead people.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (누군가가 デマ를 퍼뜨려 우리를 속이려 함). 「デマ」는 거짓 소문·허위 정보를 의미하며 'デマを流す(데마를 퍼뜨리다)'는 정형적 결합 표현입니다. 오답: 2. 그림이 가짜라는 의미는 '偽物'을 사용. 3. 나이를 잘못 알았다는 의미는 '勘違い'가 적절. 4. 두 사람이 풀어야 할 것은 '誤解(오해)'이지 デマ가 아닙니다. デマ는 사람·집단을 대상으로 의도적으로 퍼뜨리는 거짓 정보에 한정됩니다.

questions.stem: 長く使っていた古い冷蔵庫を、引っ越し（ ）買い替えた。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: を機に (をきに) is correct. It means "taking this opportunity" or "on the occasion of." Question Translation I bought a new refrigerator, taking the opportunity of moving. Explanation The phrase "を機に" is used to indicate that a certain action was taken as a result of, or on the occasion of, a particular event. In this context, the speaker decided to buy a new refrigerator because they were moving. Thus, the moving provided a convenient opportunity to replace the old refrigerator. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: を皮切りに (をかわきりに) means "starting with" or "beginning with." It is used to indicate the start of a series of events or actions and doesn’t imply taking advantage of an opportunity in this context. - Answer 3: に基づいて (にもとづいて) means "based on" or "according to." It is used when actions or decisions are grounded in certain standards, data, or information, which doesn’t fit the context of replacing an old refrigerator due to moving. - Answer 4: にわたって (にわたって) means "over" or "throughout," typically used with periods of time or extent. It does not convey the meaning of taking advantage of an opportunity provided by an event like moving. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The expression "を機に" typically follows a noun and is used to express that something was done on the occasion or opportunity presented by the event described by that noun. This is a common structure in Japanese where you leverage events to talk about consequential decisions or changes.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 1번 (を機に). '~을 계기로'의 뜻으로, 이사를 계기로 오래 쓰던 냉장고를 새로 샀다는 인과·계기 관계를 자연스럽게 표현합니다. 어떤 사건을 전환점 삼아 행동을 취할 때 쓰는 정중·격식 표현입니다. 오답: 2. を皮切りに는 '~을 시작으로'(연쇄적 시작). 3. に基づいて는 '~에 근거하여'. 4. にわたって는 '~에 걸쳐'(기간·범위)로 모두 의미가 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 経営者は、会社は社員（ ）ものであるということを忘れてはいけない。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: あっての is correct. It means "existing because of" or "owing to." Question Translation The management must not forget that a company exists because of its employees. Explanation The phrase "あっての" is used to express that something cannot exist without something else; it emphasizes the indispensable nature of the latter. In this sentence, it highlights the importance of employees in the existence of a company, suggesting that the company's existence is reliant on its employees. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: たる is a classical expression used in formal or written Japanese meaning "to be" or "as a." It does not fit the context of indicating the dependency or the fundamental basis the way あっての does. - Answer 2: におけ is used in the pattern "XにおけるY," meaning "Y at/in X," indicating location or context. It doesn't convey a dependence or essential relationship in this context. - Answer 4: ならではの means "uniquely characteristic of" or "distinctive of." While it implies a special quality, it doesn't capture the essential dependence meant here. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The grammatical pattern "あっての" is used to show that something exists or is viable because of another essential thing or condition. This pattern is vital in contexts where the complex interplay of elements and their dependencies is being discussed, particularly in Japanese business or organizational contexts.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (あっての). '~이 있어야 비로소 성립하는'의 의미로 회사는 사원이 있어야 존재한다는 의존 관계를 강조하는 문맥에 정확히 부합합니다. A あっての B의 형태로 B가 A 없이는 성립하지 않음을 강하게 단언합니다. 오답: 1. たる는 '~인 자가 마땅히 가져야 할'(자격·역할). 2. におけ는 형태가 부적합. 4. ならではの는 '~만의 독특한'으로 의존 관계와는 다릅니다.

questions.stem: 私はこの漫画が大好きで、つらいときに（ ）元気をもらっている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 読み返しては is correct. It means "each time I read it." Question Translation I love this manga, and each time I read it, I gain strength when I'm having a tough time. Explanation The phrase 読み返しては (よみかえしては) indicates a repetitive action with a resulting state or effect. It implies that the act of reading the manga (読み返す) repeatedly leads to gaining strength or motivation. This structure fits the context well because it describes a habitual action that consistently provides comfort or encouragement. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 読み返すべく (よみかえすべく) means "in order to read again." It suggests an intention or purpose, which doesn't fit the meaning of the sentence as there's no indication of action with intent for future reading. - Answer 2: 読み返すならば (よみかえすならば) means "if I read it again." This conditional form doesn't convey the idea of a habitual, reassuring action that the sentence is describing. - Answer 4: 読み返してこそ (よみかえしてこそ) can mean "only after reading it again," emphasizing a condition or requirement, which suggests a different nuance or prerequisite that's not present in the context of simply gaining strength. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The pattern "verb + ては" is often used to express a repetitive condition with a predictable result. It is frequently used to indicate habitual actions that consistently lead to certain outcomes, making it suitable for expressing behaviors or activities done regularly and yielding specific feelings or achievements.
questions.expl_ko: 정답: 3번 (読み返しては). '~할 때마다(반복적으로)'의 의미로, 만화를 다시 읽을 때마다 힘을 얻는다는 반복적 습관·결과를 나타내는 문맥에 적합합니다. 「て(で)は」 형식은 반복 행위와 그에 따르는 결과를 연결합니다. 오답: 1. 読み返すべく는 '~하기 위해'(목적). 2. 読み返すならば는 '읽는다면'(가정). 4. 読み返してこそ는 '~해야만 비로소'로 모두 반복 의미가 없습니다.

questions.stem: 連休中どこにも出かけず、（ ）、今日は朝からどうも体が怠い。
opts: 寝たことにしたせいか
寝てばかりいたせいか
寝たことにしたとすると
寝てばかりいたとすると
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 寝てばかりいたせいか is correct. It means "perhaps because I was just sleeping all the time." Question Translation During the long holiday, I didn’t go anywhere and just slept all the time, so perhaps that’s why my body feels sluggish since this morning. Explanation The sentence presents a reason or cause for feeling sluggish, which is attributed to an action (sleeping all the time) during the holiday. The structure "〜ばかりいる" indicates a repeated or continuous action, and "せいか" is used to suggest a reason or cause, often implying "perhaps because of." The sentence suggests that frequent sleeping during the holidays might be the reason for feeling sluggish, making this answer the most appropriate. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 寝たことにしたせいか means "perhaps because I pretended to sleep." This implies pretending rather than actually sleeping, which doesn’t logically connect to feeling sluggish. - Answer 3: 寝たことにしたとすると means "if we assume I pretended to sleep." This implies a hypothetical scenario that doesn’t necessarily relate to feeling sluggish and doesn’t match the structure needed to express a cause. - Answer 4: 寝てばかりいたとすると means "if we assume I was sleeping all the time." Similar to 3, it sets up a hypothetical condition rather than providing a reason for the current state and lacks causal implication. Grammar/Vocabulary Note - "〜ばかり" is often used to indicate doing something continuously or repeatedly. - "せいか" indicates a possible reason or cause,
